Advanced Code Display with Nuxt Content Components
Explore various specialized components for displaying code effectively in Nuxt Content, including collapsible code blocks, grouped examples, live previews, and file trees.
Mastering Code Presentation in Nuxt Content

Presenting code clearly and interactively is crucial for technical documentation and tutorials. Nuxt Content, especially with integrated UI libraries, offers a suite of powerful components designed specifically for this purpose. This article demonstrates
component allows you to display code blocks that can be expanded or collapsed, saving space and improving readability, especially for longer code snippets or configuration files.
component is ideal when you need to provide the same code example in different languages or for different tools/package managers. Users can easily switch between the options.

component is excellent for visualizing project structures or showing how different configuration files relate to each other. It allows users to browse through multiple files within a single interactive block.

# Nuxt 4 Minimal Starter

Look at the [Nuxt 4 documentation](https://nuxt.com/docs/getting-started/introduction) to learn more.

## Setup

Make sure to install the dependencies:

```bash
# npm
npm install

# pnpm
pnpm install

# yarn
yarn install

# bun
bun install
```

## Development server

Start the development server on `http://localhost:3000`:

```bash
# npm
npm run dev

# pnpm
pnpm run dev

# yarn
yarn dev

# bun
bun run dev
```

## Production

Build the application for production:

```bash
# npm
npm run build

# pnpm
pnpm run build

# yarn
yarn build

# bun
bun run build
```

Locally preview production build:

```bash
# npm
npm run preview

# pnpm
pnpm run preview

# yarn
yarn preview

# bun
bun run preview
```

Check out the [deployment documentation](https://nuxt.com/docs/getting-started/deployment) for more information.

Delete all local branches whose remote no longer exists

git fetch --prune
git branch -vv | grep ': gone]' | awk '{print $1}' | xargs git branch -D
git branch --merged | grep -v "^*" | xargs git branch -d

git fetch origin pull/<PR_NUMBER>/head:pr-<PR_NUMBER>
git checkout pr-<PR_NUMBER>
Delete commits from a PR (rewrite history)

# Reset to a specific commit
git reset --hard <commit>
# Rebase on the latest main branch
git fetch origin
git rebase origin/main
# Force-push to update the PR
git push -f
# Rebase on the latest main branch

git push -f

can be set as prop or in the default slot with full
support. This field represents the unique identifier for an item.

component provides a structured way to list them, making complex configurations easier to digest.

- Enables SQL database to store your application's data. Essential for dynamic content and user management.
component allows you to easily embed vector icons from various icon sets (like Lucide, Simple Icons) directly into your content, adding visual cues and enhancing aesthetics.
component is perfect for documenting keyboard shortcuts, making it clear to users which keys to press for specific actions.

def process_data(data):
    """Processes a list of numbers, returning their sum and average."""
    total = sum(data)
    average = total / len(data) if data else 0
    return total, average

numbers = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
sum_val, avg_val = process_data(numbers)
print(f"Sum: {sum_val}, Average: {avg_val}")

An in-depth look at the various lifecycle hooks available in Vue.js, with practical examples and use cases for each stage.

Vue.js instances go through a series of initialization steps when created. For example, they need to set up data observation, compile the template, mount the instance to the DOM, and update the DOM when data changes. Along the way, they also run functions called lifecycle hooks, giving users opportunities to add their own code at specific stages.
Here's a quick overview of the most commonly used lifecycle hooks:
Called synchronously after the instance has just been initialized, before data observation and event/lifecycle callbacks are set up.
Called synchronously after the instance is created. Data observation has been set up, computed properties and watch callbacks are ready, but the
// You can access data here, but not the DOM
Called right before the component is mounted to the DOM. The template has been compiled, but the component is not yet rendered to the DOM.
Called after the component has been mounted to the DOM. You can now access the component's root DOM element via
// Perfect for DOM manipulation or integrating third-party libraries
Called when data changes, before the DOM is patched.
Called after the component's DOM has been updated.
Called right before a component instance is unmounted.
Called after a component instance has been unmounted.
Consider a component that fetches data from an API.
is suitable for data fetching, while
can be used for DOM-dependent operations, and

Understanding Vue.js lifecycle hooks is crucial for building robust and efficient applications. By strategically placing your code within these hooks, you can control the behavior of your components at different stages of their existence.

Getting Started with Git and Version Control
A beginner-friendly guide to understanding Git, basic commands, and essential version control concepts for developers.
Introduction to Git and Version Control

Git is a powerful, distributed version control system that helps developers track changes in source code during software development. It's designed for coordinating work among programmers, but it can be used to track changes in any set of files.
Version control systems (VCS) are essential for several reasons:
: Multiple developers can work on the same project simultaneously without overwriting each other's changes.
: Every change made to the codebase is recorded, allowing you to revert to previous versions if needed.
: Create separate lines of development for new features or bug fixes, then integrate them back into the main codebase.
: Your code history is stored, providing a safety net against data loss.
Here are some fundamental Git commands you'll use regularly:
Creates a copy of an existing remote repository
Stages changes for the next commit
Saves staged changes to the repository
Uploads local commits to a remote repository
Fetches and integrates changes from a remote
Switches branches or restores working tree files
Let's walk through a common Git workflow for adding a new feature:
Create a new branch for your feature
git commit -m "feat: Implement my new feature"
Push your new branch to the remote repository
on GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket to merge your feature branch into the
file specifies intentionally untracked files that Git should ignore. This is useful for keeping sensitive information (like API keys) or generated files (like
Git is an indispensable tool for modern software development. Mastering its basic commands and understanding common workflows will significantly improve your productivity and collaboration skills.

Learn the fundamentals of responsive web design, including media queries, flexible grids, and fluid images, to create websites that adapt to any screen size.

In today's multi-device world, creating websites that look and function well across all screen sizes is crucial. Responsive Web Design (RWD) is an approach to web design that makes web pages render well on a variety of devices and window or screen sizes from minimum to maximum display size.
Responsive design is built upon three core principles:
) for widths and spacing instead of fixed pixel values.
: Ensuring images and other media scale proportionally to their containing elements.
: Applying different CSS styles based on device characteristics like screen width, height, resolution, and orientation.
Instead of fixed-width columns, use percentages:
To make images responsive, simply set their
/* Image will scale down if larger than its container */
Media queries allow you to apply specific styles when certain conditions are met.
/* Styles for screens wider than 768px (e.g., tablets and desktops) */
/* Styles for screens wider than 1024px (e.g., desktops) */
Test on various devices and browsers.
By embracing these principles and techniques, you can ensure your web projects provide an optimal viewing experience across the vast landscape of modern devices.

A more concise syntax for writing function expressions. They do not have their own

A Guide to Python Virtual Environments
Understand why and how to use Python virtual environments to manage project dependencies and avoid conflicts.
Python Virtual Environments: Isolation for Your Projects

When working on Python projects, you'll often encounter situations where different projects require different versions of libraries, or even different versions of Python itself. This is where Python virtual environments come in handy. A virtual environment is a self-contained directory tree that contains a Python installation for a particular version of Python, plus a number of additional packages.
: Each project can have its own set of dependencies without affecting other projects or the global Python installation.
: Prevents "dependency hell" where one project's requirements clash with another's.
: Keeps project dependencies organized and separate from your system-wide Python packages.
: Makes it easy to reproduce the exact environment for a project on another machine.
How to Create and Use Virtual Environments
The most common way to create virtual environments in modern Python is using the built-in
Navigate to your project directory and run:
This command creates a directory named
is conventional) inside your project, containing the Python executable and
Before installing packages, you need to activate the virtual environment.
Once activated, your terminal prompt will typically change to indicate that you are inside the virtual environment (e.g.,
With the environment activated, you can now install project-specific packages using
These packages will be installed only within this virtual environment.
When you're done working on the project, you can deactivate the environment:
Your terminal prompt will return to its normal state.
It's good practice to keep track of your project's dependencies.
To export a list of all installed packages and their versions to a
To install all dependencies listed in a
file (e.g., when setting up a project on a new machine):
Let's say you're starting a new web scraping project:

# 1. Create project directory
mkdir my_scraper
cd my_scraper

# 2. Create virtual environment
python -m venv venv

# 3. Activate environment
# On Windows:
# .\venv\Scripts\activate
# On macOS/Linux:
source venv/bin/activate

# 4. Install necessary packages
pip install requests beautifulsoup4

# 5. Write your Python code (e.g., scraper.py)
# ... code that uses requests and beautifulsoup4 ...

# 6. Save dependencies
pip freeze > requirements.txt

# 7. Deactivate environment
deactivate
# ... code that uses requests and beautifulsoup4 ...

Virtual environments are a fundamental tool for any Python developer. They provide a clean, isolated, and reproducible way to manage your project's dependencies, making development smoother and more reliable.

A beginner-friendly guide to Docker, covering basic concepts, commands, and how to containerize a simple application.

Docker has revolutionized how developers build, ship, and run applications. It provides a way to package applications into isolated environments called containers, ensuring consistency across different development, testing, and production environments.

Docker is a platform that uses OS-level virtualization to deliver software in packages called containers. Containers are isolated from one another and bundle their own software, libraries, and configuration files; they can communicate with each other through well-defined channels.
: A read-only template with instructions for creating a Docker container.
: A runnable instance of a Docker image.
: A script that contains instructions for Docker to build an image.
: A cloud-based registry service for sharing and managing Docker images.
Builds an image from a Dockerfile
Runs a command in a new container
Stops one or more running containers
Pulls an image or a repository from a registry
Log in to a Docker registry
Let's containerize a basic Node.js "Hello World" application.

# Use an official Node.js runtime as a parent image
FROM node:18-alpine

# Set the working directory in the container
WORKDIR /app

# Copy package.json and package-lock.json to the working directory
COPY package*.json ./

# Install any dependencies
RUN npm install

# Copy the rest of the application code to the working directory
COPY . .

# Expose the port the app runs on
EXPOSE 3000

# Define the command to run the application
CMD ["node", "app.js"]
# Use an official Node.js runtime as a parent image
# Set the working directory in the container
# Copy package.json and package-lock.json to the working directory
# Copy the rest of the application code to the working directory
# Expose the port the app runs on
# Define the command to run the application
Now, if you open your browser and navigate to
: Containers can run consistently on any machine with Docker installed.
: Applications and their dependencies are isolated, preventing conflicts.
: Containers are lightweight and start quickly.
: Easy to scale applications by running multiple instances of containers.

Docker is an invaluable tool in a modern developer's toolkit. By understanding its core concepts and commands, you can significantly streamline your development workflow and deploy applications with confidence.

A comprehensive article with many chapters, subchapters, text, code, and lists.

Welcome to this in-depth exploration of various Markdown features. This article is designed to showcase the rendering capabilities of a robust Markdown parser, including nested headings, code blocks, lists, tables, and more.
Markdown is a lightweight markup language for creating formatted text using a plain-text editor. It's widely used for writing documentation, blog posts, and even books.
You can easily format text in Markdown.
: Use double asterisks or double underscores. For example,
: Use single asterisks or single underscores. For example,
Here's an example sentence: This is some
Headers are crucial for organizing content. Markdown supports six levels of headings.
The structure of this document itself is an example of nested headings.
Lists are fundamental for presenting information in an organized manner. Markdown supports ordered and unordered lists.
Showcasing code is a common requirement. Markdown offers inline code and code blocks.
is an inline JavaScript variable declaration.
Code blocks allow for multi-line code snippets with syntax highlighting.

Welcome

  • Home
  • About
  • Contact

Blockquotes are used for quoting external content, and emphasis helps highlight text.

It can span several lines and even include
empty lines between paragraphs within the quote.

And even a third level!

Tables are excellent for presenting tabular data.
Hyperlinks and images are essential for rich content.

Demonstrating Nuxt MDC functionality with a custom component.
This article demonstrates how to use Nuxt MDC (Markdown Components) to embed Vue components directly within Markdown files.
You can see the custom component rendered above this text. This powerful feature allows for dynamic and interactive content within your static Markdown files.
Nuxt Content automatically registers components found in
(or configured paths) and makes them available in Markdown files using the
For more complex components, you can pass props:

::MyComponent
#title
This is my title.
#description
This is my description.
::

Showcasing Typography Components in Nuxt Content
An article demonstrating the usage and appearance of various typography components like accordions, badges, callouts, notes, tips, warnings, cautions, and cards within Nuxt Content.
Introduction to Nuxt Content Typography Components

Nuxt Content, when combined with a UI library like Nuxt UI, allows for the seamless integration of rich typography components directly into your Markdown files. This article serves as a showcase for some of these powerful components, demonstrating how they can be used to enhance the readability and interactivity of your content.
component is excellent for presenting collapsible sections of content, perfect for FAQs or detailed explanations that you don't want to display all at once.
Nuxt Content is a Git-based headless CMS for your Nuxt application. It allows you to write content in Markdown, JSON, YAML, CSV, or XML files and fetch it with a MongoDB-like API.
How do I use these components?
These components are typically registered globally or through auto-import in a Nuxt application, making them available directly within your Markdown files using the
Yes! Depending on your UI framework (e.g., Nuxt UI), there can be a wide array of components for various purposes, from form elements to complex data displays.
Badges are small status indicators or labels that can highlight important information, versions, or categories.
Callouts are prominent boxes used to draw attention to specific pieces of information, often with an icon and a distinct color. They can also include links.
support. It can guide users to important sections or external resources.
Informational Blocks: Note, Tip, Warning, Caution
These components provide standardized ways to present different types of informational messages, helping to categorize and emphasize content appropriately.
Here's some additional information you might find useful about component usage. Remember to check the official documentation for advanced configurations.
Here's a helpful suggestion: always keep your content files organized and use clear naming conventions for better maintainability.
Be careful when making changes to component props directly in Markdown, as incorrect values might lead to unexpected rendering issues.
This action cannot be undone. Ensure you have backed up your content before performing any large-scale refactoring or deletions.
Cards are versatile containers for content, often used to display distinct blocks of information, such as product features, team members, or linked resources.
Discover advanced components and features for building professional Nuxt applications with Nuxt UI Pro.
Cards can also be grouped together to form a visually appealing and organized collection.
A simple yet powerful blog template built with Nuxt Content.
Showcase your work and skills with this elegant portfolio template.
Build your online store with a modern e-commerce solution.

This article demonstrates just a fraction of the possibilities when combining Markdown with powerful UI components in Nuxt Content. By leveraging these elements, you can create highly structured, engaging, and user-friendly documentation and content experiences.
